coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance criteria for production backfill execution will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.

coaching.coaching: Pin down dry-run default versus idempotency second-run assertion with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: dry-run default versus idempotency second-run assertion

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for rollback mechanism for written caused_by links — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: rollback mechanism for written caused_by links

coaching.coaching: Pin down error-handling posture across the backfill loop with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: error-handling posture across the backfill loop

coaching.coaching: Plan how dry-run suppression of writes verification will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: dry-run suppression of writes verification

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on coupling risk from private API bypass: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: coupling risk from private API bypass

coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind TOCTOU race in check-then-act idempotency guard first.
coaching.subject: TOCTOU race in check-then-act idempotency guard

coaching.coaching: Plan how operational error state test coverage will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.

coaching.coaching: Realign parent epic backfill-labeling metadata contract to the parent's plan — the parent wins on conflict; if the parent is genuinely wrong, update the PARENT first (which forces its re-review), never silently diverge the leaf.

coaching.coaching: Weigh at least one structural alternative for batching and throttling strategy for closed-bug iteration.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that proves the backfill was actually executed: e.g., `python scripts/backfill_caused_by.py --dry-run` against the real (or a representative anonymized) tracker store must complete without error and print ≥1 candidate link, with the output or link count recorded on the ticket as the completion artifact.
evidence: Plan end-state: 'run the conservative blame -> commit -> ticket resolver over existing closed bugs to draw caused_by links retroactively, seeding source-of-bugs history'
Both ACs are scoped to `tests/scripts/test_backfill_caused_by.py` with mocked/stubbed fixtures — no AC covers the actual production or staging run
ABSENCE: no AC of the form 'after running against the real tracker, --dry-run output lists ≥1 candidate link' or 'after running without --dry-run, graph contains ≥1 new caused_by edge'
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria cover only the unit-test fixture, not the actual execution of the backfill script against real/production data. The stated end-state ('seeding source-of-bugs history') is never verified: no AC requires running `python scripts/backfill_caused_by.py --dry-run` against the real tracker store and asserting that N links would be drawn (or were drawn), so a passing test suite does not prove the backfill was ever run or produced any result.
scenarios: Agent merges the PR with green tests but never runs the script; the backfill never happens and the ticket is closed with no actual links drawn
Script has a bug specific to the real tracker data format that mocks don't catch; tests pass but the real run produces zero links or errors silently
findings.suggested_fix: Add a third AC such as: '[ ] Running `python scripts/backfill_caused_by.py --dry-run --tracker-dir <real_path>` exits 0 and reports ≥1 candidate `caused_by` link; after a live run (without `--dry-run`), `assert graph.caused_by_count() > 0` or equivalent query confirms links exist in the store.' Attach the dry-run output to the ticket as the completion artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between '--dry-run by default' and the idempotency AC: either clarify that the idempotency test runs in non-dry-run mode explicitly (e.g. `--write` flag), or reframe AC2 to describe dry-run idempotency semantics (proposing 1 link both times) and add a separate non-dry-run idempotency test.
evidence: '--dry-run by default so a run previews before writing'
'a second run draws 0 new links — test asserts this'
'the script checks _is_active_link before writing' — only meaningful if something was written in a prior run
If dry-run never writes, _is_active_link guard is never triggered, so idempotency via that mechanism cannot be demonstrated in dry-run mode
findings.finding: The plan states '--dry-run by default' in the Approach section, but AC2 asserts 'a second run draws 0 new links' based on _is_active_link finding an existing active link — which can only happen if the first test run actually wrote links (non-dry-run). If the test uses the default dry-run mode, nothing is ever written, _is_active_link always returns False, and every run would 'propose' 1 link, not 0. This is a genuine contradiction between the Approach section and the AC/Tests sections.
findings.location: Approach section ('--dry-run default') vs AC2 ('second run draws 0 new links') vs Tests section
scenarios: Implementer writes the test using the default dry-run flag; the second-run assertion fails because nothing was ever written and _is_active_link never returns True, making the idempotency AC untestable as described.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the Tests section that the idempotency test explicitly passes `--write` (or `--no-dry-run`) on the first run to commit links, then re-runs to assert 0 new links are written; keep the dry-run default intact but make the test's flag explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete back-out procedure to the plan: specify the command or script step to remove the additive caused_by links written by the backfill (e.g. a `--rollback` flag, a companion delete script, or the exact graph API call to remove a link event).
evidence: 'back-out is removing the additive links' — no deletion command, script, or procedure is named
The plan uses private _write_link_event directly; no corresponding delete path is described
ABSENCE: no rollback script, no graph mutation command, no manual step enumerated
findings.finding: The plan's back-out description ('back-out is removing the additive links') acknowledges reversibility but provides no mechanism — no command, no tooling, no procedure for removing written caused_by links. T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not merely an acknowledgement that removal is theoretically possible.
findings.location: Approach section: 'back-out is removing the additive links'
scenarios: After a production backfill run, a data-quality issue is discovered and the team needs to roll back — there is no documented procedure, forcing ad-hoc reverse-engineering of the graph's internal link storage format.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-bug exception handling with logged warnings (so one bad bug doesn't abort the entire backfill) and emit a final summary line (processed/linked/skipped/errored counts) for operator visibility.
evidence: No mention of try/except, retry, or per-bug error logging in the Approach or Files sections
No mention of progress/failure observability (e.g., count of skipped-due-to-error vs skipped-due-to-idempotency)
'back-out is removing the additive links' — only rollback strategy mentioned, not failure-during-forward-run
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for failures during the backfill loop — git blame errors, `_write_link_event` I/O failures, or unexpected exceptions when iterating closed bugs — leaving the script's behavior on partial failure unspecified.
scenarios: git blame fails on a corrupt or shallow-cloned repo — script crashes mid-run, leaving no record of which bugs were processed
A transient I/O error on `_write_link_event` for bug N causes silent termination; operator has no visibility into how many links were written before failure
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap each bug's processing in a try/except that logs `logger.warning('bug %s: skipped due to error: %s', bug_id, e)` and increments an error counter, then print a summary at script exit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a concrete retire/back-out mechanism: either add a `--undo` flag that removes links written by the most recent backfill run (using a session marker/tag written at backfill time), or document the exact query/command operators use to identify and delete backfill-origin links.
evidence: Plan text: 'back-out is removing the additive links' — no rollback script, no `--undo` flag, no migration step, no specification of which records to target or how to identify backfill-written vs. organically-written links
The plan explicitly bypasses `add_dependency`'s closed-source guard by writing via `_write_link_event` directly, but does not add any backfill-origin marker that would allow selective removal
findings.finding: The retire/cleanup path for backfilled `caused_by` links is described only as 'removing the additive links' with no specified mechanism, script, or command — leaving operators with no concrete back-out procedure if the backfill produces incorrect links.
findings.location: Approach section: 'back-out is removing the additive links'
scenarios: Operator discovers post-backfill that a source-code repository had a force-push that corrupted blame data, making all backfilled links incorrect; without a rollback mechanism or origin marker, they cannot distinguish backfill-written links from organic ones and must manually inspect every `caused_by` edge
findings.suggested_fix: At write time, include a metadata marker (e.g., `source=backfill_caused_by`) in the link event payload; add a `--undo` flag that deletes all link events carrying that marker, and test the undo path in `test_backfill_caused_by.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the script's error-handling posture for `CommandError` from `_write_link_event`: either fail-fast (re-runnable due to idempotency) or continue-with-warning, AND emit a final summary of written/skipped/failed counts so operators can verify completeness.
evidence: Plan: 'before writing it calls rebar.graph._links._is_active_link(…) and SKIPS the bug when an active caused_by edge to that culprit already exists'
_write_link_event docstring: 'Raises CommandError on a genuine commit failure (e.g. rebase-in-progress guard, exit 75)'
Plan ACs specify link count assertions but say nothing about write-failure behavior
findings.finding: The plan's Approach section specifies the write path (call _write_link_event after _is_active_link check) but does not specify the error-handling posture when _write_link_event raises CommandError (documented failure modes: rebase-in-progress guard exit 75, lock timeout). For a batch backfill iterating over N closed bugs, this is load-bearing: an unhandled CommandError on bug K aborts the loop and silently leaves bugs K+1…N unprocessed, while a swallowed error loses the operator's ability to know which writes failed. Neither the Approach section nor the ACs specify fail-fast vs. continue-with-warning, nor whether a summary of failed/skipped/written counts is emitted.
scenarios: Backfill runs on a repo with an in-progress rebase or merge — _write_link_event raises CommandError on the first write attempt; remaining N-1 bugs are never processed
Script aborts mid-run leaving no log of which bugs failed; re-run is idempotent but operator cannot distinguish 'skipped (already linked)' from 'failed (CommandError)'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the per-bug write in a try/except CommandError that logs a warning and increments a failure counter; emit a final summary line (e.g. 'backfill complete: 12 linked, 34 skipped (already active), 2 failed') and exit non-zero if any failures occurred. Since the script is idempotent, fail-continue is the friendlier choice for bulk backfills.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the rollback mechanism concretely (e.g. 'issue UNLINK events via `rebar unlink` for each written edge, or run `scripts/backfill_caused_by.py --undo`') and assert the post-rollback schema state including SNAPSHOT consistency.
evidence: Plan text: 'back-out is removing the additive links' — no tool, script, or command named
ABSENCE: no assertion on post-rollback schema state

src/rebar/graph/_links.py lines 42-115: _is_active_link falls back to scanning SNAPSHOT compiled_state.deps[] when *-LINK.json files are deleted by ticket-compact.sh — meaning UNLINK events may be needed rather than file deletion, and snapshot state must also be correct after rollback
T11 checklist: 'plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
findings.finding: The plan's back-out path ('back-out is removing the additive links') does not specify HOW links are removed, does not identify a rollback script or command, and does not assert the post-rollback schema state — specifically whether compiled SNAPSHOT files (which bake LINK events via ticket-compact.sh) also need invalidation after link removal.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out is removing the additive links'
scenarios: Operator runs the backfill, decides to roll back, deletes the LINK event files manually — but compacted SNAPSHOT files still contain the baked `caused_by` deps; _is_active_link returns True from the SNAPSHOT path, leaving ghost edges.
Partial failure mid-run leaves some bugs linked; operator has no mechanism to identify which links were written before the crash to issue targeted UNLINK events.

findings.finding: The rollback path 'back-out is removing the additive links' is under-specified and potentially ambiguous. LINK events in the store are immutable appended files; 'removing' them could mean (1) deleting the LINK event JSON files from disk, or (2) writing compensating UNLINK events. The plan does not specify the mechanism, does not tag backfill-written LINK events distinctively (e.g., `source=backfill`), and does not describe what the store state looks like after a partial rollback. DATA LOSS RISK: inconsistent partial rollback (e.g. some LINK files deleted, some left) leaves the graph in an incorrect intermediate state that the reducer may misread.
findings.location: Approach section — 'back-out is removing the additive links'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `'source': 'backfill_caused_by'` to the LINK event payload written by the script, then provide a `--rollback` mode that issues compensating UNLINK events for all LINK events carrying that source tag. Assert in the plan that after rollback no caused_by edges with source=backfill_caused_by remain active.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC and a test case asserting that invoking the script with `--dry-run` (or relying on the default) over a fixture with eligible bugs writes exactly 0 links to the store.
evidence: Plan states '`--dry-run` default so a run previews before writing' but neither the Tests section nor the Acceptance Criteria includes an assertion that a `--dry-run` invocation writes 0 links.
AC bullets only assert `links == 1` (write path) and idempotency (also write path); the dry-run suppression branch is untested.
ABSENCE: no AC line of the form 'running with `--dry-run` over the same fixture produces 0 written links'
findings.finding: The `--dry-run` flag (the default mode, and the stated primary safety mechanism) has no test asserting it actually suppresses writes. The only AC-level tests cover the write path; a broken `--dry-run` that silently writes would pass the entire test suite.
scenarios: A regression introduces a bug where `--dry-run` writes links anyway; the test suite stays green because no test checks the dry-run output.
An operator runs the script in production expecting a preview, gets irreversible writes instead because the guard was silently broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '`--dry-run` mode (the default): running against the same two-bug fixture with `--dry-run` active writes 0 links and instead prints/returns the proposed links — test asserts store link count remains 0 after the call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying that the default (no flags) execution produces preview output and writes zero links to the graph store.
evidence: '--dry-run by default so a run previews before writing'
AC1 asserts 'links == 1' (actual or proposed) but does not verify the dry-run default specifically
AC2 asserts idempotency but does not verify dry-run behavior
ABSENCE: no AC of the form 'running without --write produces no graph mutations'
findings.finding: The --dry-run default behavior is described in the Approach section as a key safety feature, but neither acceptance criterion verifies it. There is no AC checking that running without an explicit flag produces preview output without writing any links, leaving this described deliverable uncovered.
findings.location: Approach section ('--dry-run by default') vs Acceptance Criteria section (no covering criterion)
scenarios: Implementer ships the script; the dry-run default is accidentally broken or inverted; no test catches it because no AC covers this behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] Running `python scripts/backfill_caused_by.py` with no flags (dry-run default) produces proposed-link output but writes 0 links — verified by asserting the graph store is unchanged after a dry run over the two-bug fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the decision to bypass `add_dependency` via `_write_link_event` in an ADR or AGENTS.md entry, and consider extracting a named internal helper or escape-hatch function to make the coupling explicit and reduce future breakage risk.
evidence: 'a closed-bug backfill must write via it, bypassing `add_dependency`'s closed-source guard'
Direct use of `rebar.graph._links._write_link_event` and `rebar.graph._links._is_active_link` (private internal APIs, leading-underscore convention)
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry for this bypass decision
findings.finding: The plan directly invokes private APIs `rebar.graph._links._write_link_event` and `rebar.graph._links._is_active_link` to bypass `add_dependency`'s closed-source guard, but does not acknowledge the coupling risk, propose any mitigation (wrapper function, interface boundary), or capture this architectural decision in an ADR or design doc update.
scenarios: A future refactor of rebar.graph._links internals silently breaks the backfill script with no indication at the interface level
Another developer writes a second backfill or maintenance script and re-discovers this bypass pattern independently, creating drift
findings.suggested_fix: Add a thin package-internal function (e.g., `rebar.graph._links.write_link_unchecked(...)`) that the backfill calls by name, and note in AGENTS.md or an ADR why the closed-source guard is intentionally bypassed for backfill.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the idempotency guard atomic: either acquire a file-level lock around the check+write pair, use an atomic rename/append pattern, or document a concurrency constraint (e.g., 'only one writer at a time') with an enforcement mechanism.
evidence: Plan text: 'the script guards the write itself — before writing it calls `rebar.graph._links._is_active_link(bug_id, culprit_id, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` and SKIPS the bug when an active `caused_by` edge to that culprit already exists'
This is a classic check-then-act TOCTOU: no mention of file lock, advisory lock, or atomic rename/transaction around the check+write pair
The plan also says the live auto-derivation uses the same low-level `_write_link_event` writer, creating a second concurrent writer path
findings.finding: The idempotency guard uses a check-then-act pattern (`_is_active_link` check followed by `_write_link_event` write) with no atomic commit, lock, or CAS, creating a TOCTOU race: two concurrent backfill runs (or a backfill run concurrent with live auto-derivation) can both observe the link absent, then both write it, producing duplicate `caused_by` link events.
findings.location: Approach section: 'before writing it calls `rebar.graph._links._is_active_link(…)` and SKIPS the bug when an active `caused_by` edge to that culprit already exists'
scenarios: Operator runs backfill script twice in parallel (e.g., accidentally in two terminals): both processes check `_is_active_link` for the same bug, both get False, both call `_write_link_event`, resulting in two duplicate link events for the same caused_by relationship
Backfill script runs concurrently with live auto-derivation on a CI server that has just closed a bug: both paths write a `caused_by` link for the same (bug, culprit) pair
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `_is_active_link` + `_write_link_event` pair inside a file lock (e.g., `filelock.FileLock(tracker_dir / 'backfill.lock')`) so concurrent runs serialize; alternatively, make `_write_link_event` itself idempotent by de-duplicating on read at query time rather than at write time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit non-happy-path handling and at least one test for operational error states: specify (and test) behavior when git blame fails for a bug (log-and-skip vs. abort), and when the tracker_dir write raises a permission error, so operators have predictable script behavior and a safe re-run story.
evidence: Plan text: 'over a fixture of two closed bugs (one single-culprit, one ambiguous), the script proposes exactly one `caused_by` link'
No mention of error handling for: git not installed or blame failing, permission denied on tracker_dir write, a bug's commit SHA missing from the local clone (shallow clone, rebased history)
The ACs only assert `links == 1` and idempotency; no AC covers error-state exit behavior or user-facing error messages
findings.finding: The plan's test and non-happy-path coverage addresses only the ambiguous-culprit case; operational error states — git blame tool unavailable, tracker_dir not writable, a referenced commit absent from local history — are not handled or tested, leaving operators with no specified behavior when the script encounters these conditions.
scenarios: Operator runs backfill on a CI server with a shallow clone (--depth=1); blame.py cannot resolve commits older than the clone depth and throws an exception; with no specified error handling the script may crash mid-run, leaving the store in a partially-written state

Operator runs backfill against a read-only tracker_dir (e.g., mounted read-only NFS in a server deployment); `_write_link_event` raises PermissionError; with no specified error handling the script aborts without reporting which bugs were processed vs. skipped

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation mechanism: e.g., name the downstream consumer (e.g., 'the blame-analytics dashboard' or 'the weekly source-of-bugs report'), and add an AC or post-run check that shows an observable improvement (e.g., 'X% of closed bugs now carry a caused_by link before vs. after backfill').
evidence: 'seeding source-of-bugs history' — implied benefit but no named beneficiary (team, role, or downstream consumer) and no measurable outcome
ACs are purely system-internal: `assert links == 1` and second run draws 0 — no user-facing or operational signal
No before/after workflow comparison or metric target stated
findings.finding: The plan names no specific stakeholder or problem owner and provides no concrete validation mechanism beyond internal unit-test link-count assertions — no before/after workflow comparison, operational metric, or dogfooding step shows an observable improvement to any user.
scenarios: A reviewer cannot determine whether the backfill actually improved any stakeholder's experience or downstream analytic query, making it hard to judge when the script has 'done its job' operationally.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What or Acceptance Criteria section, add: 'Stakeholder: the blame-analytics pipeline / engineers triaging regressions. Success signal: after a non-dry-run, query the tracker store and assert that at least N previously-unlinked closed bugs now carry a caused_by edge (log the before/after count).'

findings.finding: The plan asserts idempotency ('skips bugs already carrying a caused_by') but the only write path available for closed-bug backfill — `_write_link_event` — is explicitly documented 'no idempotency'. `add_dependency`, which IS idempotent via `_is_active_link`, raises a `ValueError` on closed source tickets ('cannot create {relation} link — source ticket is closed'). The plan never names `_is_active_link` as a guard or specifies how idempotency is achieved, leaving this a codebase assertion that the tools refute when taken at face value.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Idempotent (skips bugs already carrying a `caused_by`)'; Files section: `scripts/backfill_caused_by.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files section for `scripts/backfill_caused_by.py`, add: 'skips tickets where `rebar.graph._is_active_link(bug_id, *, "caused_by", tracker_dir)` returns True (the idempotency guard); writes via `rebar.graph._links._write_link_event` (same path as `_apply_caused_by`) since `add_dependency` raises on closed-source tickets.'

findings.finding: The plan claims idempotency ('skips bugs already carrying a caused_by') but does not specify the detection mechanism. The underlying _write_link_event is documented as having 'no idempotency'; a re-run where derive_caused_by returns the same culprit will silently write a second LINK event (reducer accumulates two deps entries for the same target+relation with distinct UUIDs). The plan must specify that the script reads reduced state (state['deps']) or calls _is_active_link before invoking _write_link_event so the idempotency check is a real read-then-conditionally-write, not an unspecified assumption.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'Idempotent (skips bugs already carrying a caused_by)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Before writing, the script checks for an existing `caused_by` dep via `rebar.graph._links._is_active_link(bug_id, culprit, "caused_by", tracker_dir)`; if the link already exists it is counted as skipped (0 new links).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Realign the leaf to the parent's mandatory labeling contract: add `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified` to the `_write_link_event` call in the Approach section, and add an AC (or extend AC1) asserting that the written edge carries those fields, satisfying parent AC5.
evidence: Parent Success Criterion: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified` (the single backfill vocabulary — there is no `backfill_structural`), and `is_authoritative()` returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series.'

Parent AC5: 'Backfill is validated: at least one backfilled `caused_by`/`close_class`/snapshot value exists carrying `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`, asserted by a test over the backfill output.'

ABSENCE: The leaf's Approach section describes the write path (`_write_link_event`) and idempotency check (`_is_active_link`) but mentions neither `source=backfill_classified` nor `confidence=classified` as fields to be written.
Leaf AC1 asserts only `assert links == 1`; leaf AC2 asserts only `second run draws 0 new links` — neither asserts metadata on written edges.
The leaf's Files description for `scripts/backfill_caused_by.py` says 'draw `caused_by` on high-confidence single culprits' with no mention of provenance labels.
findings.finding: The leaf plan omits the parent's mandatory backfill-labeling contract: the parent epic requires every backfilled `caused_by` edge to carry `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`, with `is_authoritative()` returning False, AND requires a test over the backfill output asserting those labels (parent AC5 explicitly names `caused_by`). The leaf's Approach, Tests, and both ACs contain no mention of these labels or that assertion.
findings.location: Leaf: Approach section, Acceptance Criteria (both ACs) / Parent AC5 and Success Criteria (backfill labeling paragraph)
scenarios: The backfill script writes `caused_by` edges without `source`/`confidence` metadata; parent AC5 integration test fails because no backfilled value carries the required labels.

Backfilled `caused_by` links are inadvertently aggregated into authoritative structural series because `is_authoritative()` is never checked — breaking the parent's non-authoritative segregation guarantee.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'Each written `caused_by` edge is passed `source="backfill_classified"` and `confidence="classified"` so `is_authoritative()` returns False and the edge is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series.' In the Acceptance Criteria, add or extend AC1: 'The written edge carries `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified`, asserted via `assert link_metadata["source"] == "backfill_classified"` and `assert link_metadata["confidence"] == "classified"`.' This satisfies parent AC5 for the `caused_by` backfill path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add batching/throttling to the backfill script (e.g. a `--batch-size` parameter with a configurable inter-batch sleep) and document it in the plan's Approach section, so the migration does not run as one unbounded transaction.
evidence: Plan text: 'iterate closed bugs, reuse src/rebar/metrics/blame.py, draw caused_by on high-confidence single culprits'
ABSENCE: no batch size, throttle, chunk, or sleep parameter mentioned anywhere in the plan
_write_link_event routes through append_event → write_and_push (atomic rename + git commit + best-effort push) per event, confirmed in src/rebar/graph/_links.py lines 118-149
findings.finding: The plan specifies no batching or throttling for the backfill iteration. 'Iterate closed bugs' is one unbounded pass that fires `git blame` subprocesses and a git commit+push per successful culprit, with no chunk size, sleep, or rate-limit described — this can saturate I/O and block the git working tree for the full duration on a large store.
scenarios: A repo with thousands of closed bugs runs the script; each bug fires git blame over multiple files and a git commit+push, holding the fcntl lock continuously and potentially timing out or starving concurrent writers for minutes.
A partial run is interrupted and the operator must re-run; without a progress checkpoint the full cost is re-paid (though idempotency prevents double-writes, the compute cost is not amortized).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--batch-size N` (default e.g. 50) and `--sleep-between-batches S` (default 0) flag; the script commits a batch, sleeps, then continues. Document these in the plan Approach section.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
